turns.text: So then why isn't Justice White's question pertinent? There must have been a finding if they didn't believe that when he said it.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: You've already said that that support to constitution doesn't mean that I will not disagree.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: You've already --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Of course.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: I mean the -- the gloss you put on that is that -- what it means is that the man will not abide by the constitution, would disobey it.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Now he says, I don't mean that. I didn't mean that and I don't mean it.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think that was for evidence for the House to consider.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: General, you have told us that you're relying on the section of the constitution which requires every state legislator to take the oath to support the constitution of the United States. Now, is there anything in the constitution that gives a right to anyone to judge the sincerity of a legislator who is willing to take that oath?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: No sir. May I read the provision of the U.S. Constitution, its very short, Article VI, Clause 3. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the Members of the several State Legislatures, and all executives and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several States, shall be bound by Oath or Affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification, a qualification to any Office or public trust under the United States.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: My question --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- to you is that do you -- that section, if an elected officer is willing to and offers to take that oath, is there anything in the constitution of the United States that authorizes anyone under any circumstances, to pass upon his sincerity in taking that oath?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Not in the United States Constitution sir, except in that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And then I would like to know why -- how you jump from that to the fact that the legislature of Georgia has a right to test the sincerity of this man in taking that oath?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Well, may it please the Court. I have to state my position to that.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: But we -- of course this -- he was -- one of the allegation, one of the challenge unopposed stated that he was not qualified under this provision of the U.S. Constitution and --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Who said that?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: One of the challenges to Mr. Bond's being seated. And the Georgia --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You mean, he judged that the -- he passed judgment on Mr. Bond to the effect that Mr. Bond would not -- was not sincere in taking that oath.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think that would be a fair statement, yes sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think it would be. But the provisions for the House to judge it -- the Georgia House of course is not in the constitution. That relates back to the Georgia constitution, the provision that the House shall be that sole judge of the qualification in election of its members.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But now what -- you told us that there is nothing in the Georgia Constitution that directly confers with his taking the oath, have you not?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: The provision of the Georgia Constitution itself which the oath proscribes in the constitution requires to a person to be bound to support the constitution of the State of Georgia and of the United States.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, I will -- I asked you the same question then about the Georgia Constitution, is there anything in it that which authorizes anyone to pass upon the sincerity of a person who takes the oath as prescribed by the constitution?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Not in those words Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well then where -- what do you refer to in the Georgia Constitution that authorizes -- the legislature to pass upon the sincerity of this man in his willingness to take the oath?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: So the only thing that I can assume -- I hope we understand, the legislature acts independently. The legislature found authority under the qualification provision of the Georgia Constitution, that they were sole judge of the qualifications of a member to that House.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I suppose he would say that the Georgia Constitution at least is construed by the federal court, the majority of the federal court here versus the Georgia law.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Oh, yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Have you got any state court decisions on this?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: As to qualifications?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: We have no state court decisions as construed in this provision of the federal constitution.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, I should suppose that we -- you would go even further than my Brother Harlan suggest and say that the questions of Georgia law are none of the business of this Court and we have to assume that when the state legislature proceeded as it did, it was expressing what the law of Georgia is. And the only questions for us is, is whether the action of that state legislature violated the United States Constitution.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Isn't that --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think that would be a fair statement.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Don't you also say that it's not of the judiciary's business any way. I would think you do if it's a political question.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Well, later on in my argument I --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: -- get -- touch on that issue as to the due process. So it is our position that the federal constitution provides a minimum qualification, the only qualification there. May I go back one moment because of course I digress -- you might -- the Court might say is, this is a -- why, why does the U.S. Constitution, why is that important? Well, why is a state legislature, why is that important? We have 50 states and I've said in the state legislature and why is that an important position? Well, may I just very briefly state that a state legislator doesn't just make laws that affect the people in his district. A state legislator makes laws which affect the people of the entire state of which he is a member without the record modes of transportation now. A state legislator makes laws which affect the people of the entire nation. But not only that and to have a constitutional form of government, there is a single member of a state legislature could theoretically be or could theoretically cares the deciding vote in -- as to whether or not the United States Constitution itself, an amendment thereto was ratified. So it is an important office. Now when Mr. Bond -- when the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee issued this statement on January the 6th, Mr. Bond made his statements on it and then the legislature of the House convened on January the 5th. Mr. Bond was an elected representative which we maintain with all except the foul qualification that of being bound to support the constitution of the United States. There is a necessity for free speech under the constitution. I don't think I need to repeat that to this Court. It ensures the process of orderly government. This Court has imposed limitations on free speech, the known line in cases of obscenity and in the advocacy of law of violation. It is our position that Mr. Bond in adapting the statement of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee and in his statements thereon advocated violation of the Selective Service Act. And therefore his speech was not protected but even assuming that his speech was protected, we have to remember Mr. Bond's position, he was not a citizen. He was a -- in a position of a public servant making comments reflecting upon his qualifications, the qualifications imposed by the Federal Constitution. The Georgia House, under the Georgia Constitution is the sole judge of his qualification. And we think that because Mr. Bond of his elective position, he had a great responsibility and obligation than that of an ordinary citizen and that was the qualification which he was to be bound to support the United States Constitution. And therefore we say, that in this instance and under these circumstances, the Sixth Article, Article VI of the Constitution is of equal rank and dignity with the Bill of Rights of the First Amendment to the Constitution. And that the legislature in acting as a judge of his qualifications was authorized to treat Article VI of the federal constitution as controlling insofar as determining his qualifications.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Is there been any -- to your knowledge, is there been any debate in the Georgia legislature about the war in Vietnam? Is there any member of the Georgia legislature --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I'm certain there has been but I have no --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Is there any member of the Georgia legislature who has ever expressed himself as opposed to our participation in that war to your knowledge?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I don't -- I do not know Your Honor. I do not know possibly there has been.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Because I understand your argument to comes peerlessly close perhaps I misunderstood you, it may come peerlessly close to suggesting that if a legislator suggest such views then he is not qualified to take his oath even if he's subsequently reelected and getting -- supporting with question of removal he is --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: In --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- subsequently reelected and presents himself to take his oath. He is opposed to the war in Vietnam or that -- that'd be grounds for refusing the -- to allow him to take the oath.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: In the abstract if I may, I certainly don't intended it, conveyed of this Court, the impression that no one can have contrary views. But I have to relate it back to Article VI of the constitution that --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I understand that sir but don't you have to help us a little bit my --because I understand your position, this case in principle is as if Mr. Bond set out here to take the oath and at the same time he said, "I am not going to observe the oath, I am not going to obey the constitution of the United States". That it seems to me is the principle that you're presenting to us, right?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Now, don't you have to tell us just where Mr. Bond made such a statement that you alleged to be contrary to the oath, as to where it is and what precisely you're relying on? You read -- I know you referred the two passages but -- and if those are all that you're relying on, well, we ought to know that.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Well, I will cover that in -- on the next page in just a moment --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: -- in the record that we have Your Honor. We state that the Georgia House was the judge of Mr. Bond's qualification. It was sitting as a sole tribune or judicial body who were authorized to determine his qualifications. We say however that the Court should protect Mr. Bond's constitutional rights but should not substitute its judgment for the House in judging qualifications. Now back to question Justice Fortas, this statement by the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee of course which I -- I'm not going to read this to the Court because it's of -- about two pages long and my time is getting very close.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That's on page 137, that --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- statement to which you refer. Right.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Mr. Bond was called before the House of committee and on page of 111 of the record, the statement would be found that Mr. Bond stated that he was eager and anxious to encourage people not to participate in the war in Vietnam for any reason that they choose. Now, this was evidenced for the House to consider in judging Mr. Bond's qualification. He also said -- the record page 41, "I do not advocate that people should break the law". That was evidenced in his favor but it was also evidence that the House could consider. He said from the record on page 112, "I don't think that I as a second class citizen of the United States have a requirement to support the law". This also was evidenced that the House could consider.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: What is that statement mean to you? He has no obligation to support the war. Or does it mean that no obligation to agree with it? Does that mean to you that he was not -- that he would break the law in order no to support it?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Sir, I hate to answer you in this manner but I don't think it's important -- is to what it means to me as what it could have meant to the people who were sitting as his judges at that time --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, he has (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: -- for his qualification.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- to take the next sentence?

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Do you know what it meant to him?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: No sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: They have to take that next sentence though Mr. Attorney General, don't you on page 112 after saying, as second class citizen, I don't think that I have a requirement to support that or he went on to say, "I think my responsibility is to oppose things that I think are wrong if they are in Vietnam or New York". Is that correct?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes sir, wherever --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: And so there is some ambiguity there --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: There is --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- as precisely with (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I would concede that, yes Your Honor. I can't say to this Court --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: You're not telling us that you think the -- or are you, that you think the constitution imposes upon him a requirement to support the so-called war because as long as we're talking about constitution there is not a war in Vietnam, Congress has not declared war.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Are you telling us that -- with reference to what appears on page 112 that the United States Constitution imposes upon Julian Bond and on every state legislator and on every member of the United States Senate and House Representatives to support this "war" in Vietnam?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: No sir, I don't think that I could say that it's imposed in all its citizen's right to support the war in Vietnam.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Or every legislator or -- you're differentiating legislators from citizens.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes I am.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And I don't understand why (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I don't say that that statement taken in and of itself, may it please the Court, would authorize it but I think that the legislature was a judge of his qualification.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, he said here, I don't think that I as a second class citizen of the United States have a requirement to support that war. I supposed --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think he as a --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- as a second class or a first class or any other kind of a citizen, he -- there's no requirement to support that, our present foreign policy in Southeast Asia, is there?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: No sir, I agree with the Court. When the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You agree with the Court but you think that the legislature did not and that therefore they have the right to exclude Mr. Bond?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Sir I think -- if I can just summarize. I think that the legislature was a judge of his qualification, a 184 members in the vote thought he wasn't qualified, 12 members voted to seat him. What the standard of review, I think that the lower court was correct in stating a rational basis was the standard of view -- of review. I don't think we're concern with whether or not another forum might have judged Bond differently but we are concerned with the fact, was there are any evidence upon which the Georgia House could form a rational basis for finding him disqualified under the United States Constitution.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But don't we also have the problem of determining whether the House have the right to judge him on this issue?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now that --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: That is correct Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now -- alright, what to -- what is their authority for that?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Well Your Honor, we say that the provision of the constitution itself is a qualification, the federal constitution. And that the House, the Georgia House of Representatives under the Georgia Constitution is a sole judge of the qualification of its members and if therefore the House had a right to judge of his qualification and was the only body that could judge of his qualification as contained in the United States Constitution.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You've said they had this sole right to judge of his qualification?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: We say that that is a qualification, may it please the Court.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Even though it's the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I'm not trying to avoid the issue (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Even though they imposed an unconstitutional condition?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Now, if they do it in an unconstitutional manner --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do it in an unconstitutional manner, I suppose they --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: If they --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: They say that you are not qualified because of something about you that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Because they don't like the way (Inaudible) your hair.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Yes, what about that?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Well, I don't think it would be constitutional and I think this Court could so hold (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible) but supposed he said, I'm going to get the whole of this School Desegregation laws (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think it would -- wouldn't be -- have a due process, excuse me.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I would supposed to all of these School Desegregation Laws, we shouldn't obey them then and his right to speech on that, would that require --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think part of it would be possible.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: That if he advocated that other people violate the law then it would be possible to someone to -- and not choose and oppose the challenge to his qualifications.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Then they could put him out on that -- they have been able --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: After the legislature heard it, yes sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: There hasn't any other done it yet or were there?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: No sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, General I --

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I still (Inaudible) from the Governor's brief that it is a fact.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I understood you to say that if they decided that he wasn't entitled to take his seat because he parted his hair wrong that that would be for an unconstitutional reasons, would that give us jurisdiction to determine whether they have properly ejected him or not?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think that under the decisions of this Court, that this Court in such an instance as that --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Could what?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Could take jurisdiction, yes under such instances as that.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Where is that dividing line between our jurisdiction and no jurisdiction in judging of the actions of the legislature in this regard?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Well sir as I understand the law would be determined as to whether or not a person's constitutional rights had been violated.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Then we would have jurisdictions?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes sir, as I understand the decisions of this Court.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And if we thought that the legislature had no right to judge of the sincerity of the man in taking the oath, then we would have a right to say so.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: I think Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And nullify the action of the legislature.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes, I think so. If I may just summarize briefly.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: We say that the qualification imposed in the constitution was judged by the Georgia House. We don't think Mr. Bond's rights under the First Amendment were violated because we think that the speech -- parts of the speech which he made could be reasonably interpreted as advocating violation of the Selective Service Act. But even if he -- it'd be determined to the speech of Mr. Bond would have been protected as an ordinary citizen, the qualification imposed by the United States Constitution has equaled dignity with the First Amendment and is not controlling and the House was authorized to determine his qualification. We think that Mr. Bond was afforded due process. His constitutional rights were protected as there was some evidence upon which the Georgia House could make its determination of his qualification.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: But Mr. Attorney General, as I understand your previous argument, the statement that now that Mr. Bond advocated violation of Selective Service Act, doesn't seem to be supported by anything in the record. Do you -- with -- what is your contention, that it is or is not (Voice Overlap)?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Well, it's our contention Your Honor that when he said I -- urged citizens to -- excuse me, I have a reference to it right here to -- he was eager and anxious -- the record page on 111. Eager and anxious -- was he -- eager and anxious to encourage people not to participate in it, the war, for any reason that they choose.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Do you say that that is advocating violation of the Selective Service Act?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: And that's the provisional on which you're relying?

turns.name: ARTHUR K. BOLTON
turns.text: Yes, we would on that, yes sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Boudin.

turns.name: LEONARD B. BOUDIN
turns.text: Thank you. Mr. Moore will do the rebuttal Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Very well.

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: If it please the Court. We'll be extremely brief. I'd like to call the Court's attention to record page 116 in the following language of Mr. Bond.

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir. With respect to what he meant by his statement in his position. He says, "I have suggested that congressionally outline alternatives to military draft to extend it to building democracy at home. The posture of my life for the past five years has been calculated to give me a grip, the ability to participate the formulation of public policies. The fact of my election to public office does not lessen my duty or desire to express my opinions even when they differ from those held by others". I think that statement is very significant, it was before the House of Representatives as to what Mr. Bond meant. Additionally, with respect to our (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Or then he went on to say that I cannot personally participate in the war, no more.

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir, he reiterate it, his pacifist beliefs at that point.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: He reiterated what?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: His pacifist beliefs, his own objections to war. But with respect to others, he had in mind encouraging them to seek congressional alternatives to the draft and the Court is familiar with the fact that there are congressional alternatives to the draft, congressionally provided alternatives to the draft.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: There are bills proposed or there any (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: The -- and their right, our (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Our Selective Service law, a person doesn't have a right to say, "Well, I'd rather in the Peace Corps if the Selective Service Act (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: There is alternative service, the Peace Corps is one I think that this statement (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Not under present law, is there?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That what it seems to me.

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: Under the conscientious objective statute (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Oh yes.

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: The other point --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, what basis would Mr. Bond can -- if he were drafted then saying, "I will not go"?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: His own beliefs would be the only basis. He may have an out by selecting service into safe -- for example the public health service as an alternative to military service or --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Could you think he could refuse to go to war and still not break the law?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: Well he'd had to be convicted and that would be a different thing. Does that --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, what I mean --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: He could be -- he could not go off to war and still be obeying the law?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: But he could select one of the alternative services. Noncombatant military service or service in the forestry service or service in the public health or some other congressionally provided alternative to military draft.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well Mr. Justice Clark just suggested, is a pacifist, he could claim this (Inaudible), wouldn't he?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And that would solve that problem, wouldn't it?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: That would be adequate, yes sir. The other point (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did he indicate (Inaudible)?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: He did indicate that he was a pacifist.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: No, but did he indicate anywhere that he would serve or thought he should say --

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: That's not indicated by the record.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And he in effect --

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: No sir. That he should serve and the question was not put to him as to whether or not he should serve. He never -- he's never refused to serve. He did indicate that he had a draft card and he did produced it -- offered to produce the draft card at the legislative hearing.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, he pretty much indicated that he would not participate in the war. He said, "I cannot participate in war".

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: There's no question about that. The other thing that I would like to --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, then wouldn't he -- in order not to -- he wouldn't participate in the war, he would have to have some other recognized excuses for not going. On the conscientious objection that's beyond a religious basis, doesn't it?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: Not necessarily, not under the decision of this Court in the Siegel (ph) case but -- perhaps it have --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Perhaps it has anything to do with religion?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: It would have to have some religious training and belief but not necessarily a belief in the Supreme Being. If you could prove --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Still has to be on a religious basis?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: Some religious influence on the decision. It appears from the decisions of a court. Some religious influence that --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: More than --

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: -- well, as to religious belief.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: More than conscience?

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: But that does -- didn't the draft clause permit a registrant to say, "Well, I will serve in the public health service or the forestry service", or these others?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: That's my understanding of the law sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But as I understand his statement, it seems to me like the firm authorize a statement, he is against that law, he didn't want to have anything to do with it. That he doesn't intend to have anything to do with the church. It seems to me like that's a potential he has that he -- it's a view he has. Are you depending now on the ground that this statement can be construed in some fashion as meaning that he doesn't want that law violated?

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: I don't quite understand the question but I'm not defending on that ground. I'm defending on the ground that he has a right under the First Amendment to express those views and not could be excluded from the legislature.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I rather thought his statement sounded like he is proud of his views.

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: HOWARD MOORE, JR.
turns.text: If I may move on to the note which we'd like to conclude, is that the State, the respondents construe Article VI provision with respect to the oath to be synonymous with support of the Government or support of a particular administration. The Article itself provide support of the constitution and that's a very meaningful and such as to the distinction that one can't support the constitution in good faith unconscientiously and not support the particular policies of a given administration. And that dramatizes what I think has been an unconstitutional application of that provision in this particular case. Thank you.
